Kaggle · Gemma 4 Good Hackathon · Technical Submission

AniBot

Engineering the Field: How a Raspberry Pi, a Local Language Model, and a Knowledge Database Are Bringing Farming Plans to Rural Philippines

Rice & Corn Advisory Gemma 4 via Ollama Offline-First · Kiosk-Ready May 2026
AniBot is not a chatbot that reads farming PDFs. It is a local AI planning system — a disciplined stack where a Gemma 4 language model, a retrievable agricultural knowledge base, and a deterministic safety planner each do exactly what they are best at, nothing more.
The Problem Space

A Farmer Shouldn't Need Wi-Fi to Know When to Plant

In the rural Philippines, the gap between agricultural knowledge and the farmer who needs it is measured not in kilometers, but in connectivity. Municipal Agriculture Offices hold expertise. Philippine National Standards and Bureau of Agricultural and Fisheries Standards documents carry the technical detail. But access to these resources remains uneven, and the farmers who need them most are often furthest from a reliable internet connection.

AniBot was designed around one constraint: the system must work without the internet. A farmer walks into a Municipal Agriculture Office, sits at a kiosk, answers a short guided form, and receives a printed, beginner-friendly rice or corn farming plan — one that cites its sources. No account. No app download. No cloud dependency.

System Architecture

Four Layers, One Device, Zero Cloud

The architecture is purposefully lean: a FastAPI + Jinja2 web server, a SQLite knowledge database, an Ollama runtime hosting the Gemma 4 model, and a deterministic planning layer — all running on a single Raspberry Pi 5. The browser UI is the only thing a farmer sees.

Layer 1 — Local Knowledge Database. Agricultural PDFs are ingested offline, chunked with page-boundary awareness, tagged with metadata (crop, advisory_tier, allowed_use, reviewed), and stored in SQLite FTS5. Every chunk carries a page-level citation. No knowledge enters the system without a traceable source.

Layer 2 — Retrieval Engine. On a plan request, the retrieval layer queries for relevant rice or corn evidence. It separates advisory chunks (eligible to drive recommendations) from context-only documents like rainfall bulletins, which support framing but cannot become the basis for fertilizer rates.

Layer 3 — Deterministic Planner. Python code constructs a source-backed draft plan — a structured calendar with periods, tasks, how-to steps, observation prompts, decision tables, and MAO escalation triggers. This draft exists independent of the AI layer. It is the technical floor.

Layer 4 — Gemma 4 via Ollama. Gemma receives the draft and improves it. Its task is narrow: make the calendar steps clearer and more actionable for a beginner farmer, without altering dates, inventing inputs, or introducing unverified advice.

The Gemma 4 Integration

Gemma Improves, It Does Not Decide

This distinction is the core architectural claim of AniBot, and it is what separates the system from a simple RAG chatbot wrapper.

The prompt Gemma receives is a compact, structured JSON object containing: the farmer's crop, location, farming type, water source, soil condition, language preference, target date, and current concerns; retrieved evidence snippets with citations; draft calendar rows from the deterministic planner; the required output JSON schema; and explicit safety prohibitions.

Gemma is told what it cannot do: invent fertilizer rates, cite pesticide brands, add chemical dosages, alter calendar dates, or include budget guidance. What remains is the task it performs well — turning technically accurate but terse advisory language into steps a first-time farmer can follow.

The model defaults are ANIBOT_OLLAMA_HOST=http://127.0.0.1:11434 and ANIBOT_OLLAMA_MODEL=gemma4:e2b. The home page surfaces Ollama and model status in real time. If either check fails, plan generation is blocked — not silently degraded.

Knowledge Engineering

From PDF Standards to Traceable Local Retrieval

Building a usable AI knowledge base from government agricultural PDFs is harder than it sounds. Standard PDF text extraction loses heading context, making a chunk about pest thresholds indistinguishable from a general soil chapter without careful structural parsing. AniBot's ingestion pipeline solves this with heading-aware chunking that preserves the document structure that makes citations meaningful.

Every retrieved chunk surfaces a document title, page number, and section heading. When Gemma cites "Rice GAP, page 12 · 4.1 Scouting," a judge, an agronomist, or a farmer's extension worker can open the source document and verify it. Traceability is end-to-end — from original PDF to printed plan.

Safety Architecture

Three Checks Before a Plan Reaches a Farmer

A farming recommendation that invents a fertilizer rate, cites an unregistered pesticide, or suggests a chemical dosage without a source does not just fail technically — it can cause crop loss or health harm. AniBot's validation layer enforces this with no exceptions.

The Safety Validator runs three sequential checks: Pydantic schema validation (structural correctness of the JSON output), rule-based content checks (blocking invented rates, brand names, dosages, cost estimates, and altered dates), and citation grounding (confirming key recommendations map to retrieved evidence chunks).

If any check fails, the Gemma output is rejected and the deterministic draft is used as fallback. The farmer receives a plan either way. The LLM rejection is logged. This design makes Gemma genuinely safe for a municipal deployment without neutering its contribution.

Engineering Challenges

What We Solved That the Demo Doesn't Show

01
PDF → Structured Knowledge

Raw PDF extraction loses headings and page context. Built a manifest-driven ingestion pipeline with heading-aware chunking and page-level citation tagging stored in SQLite FTS5.

02
LLM Scope Creep

Early prompts asked Gemma to generate the full plan. Result: malformed JSON, missing fields, and read timeouts. Narrowed Gemma's task to improving a pre-built deterministic draft.

03
Vague Model Output

Gemma occasionally returned weak steps like "prepare seeds." Implemented a quality check: if Gemma's output is weaker than the deterministic draft on key fields, the draft is preserved.

04
Beginner Usability

Early outputs read like safety checklists. Redesigned to produce calendar rows with concrete how-to steps, decision tables, a materials checklist, MAO escalation questions, and a glossary.

05
Kiosk Practicality

A browser-only plan has limited field utility. Added server-side plan persistence with retrieval by ID, plus browser print support that targets paper or PDF export via the OS print dialog.

06
Scope Focus

Early designs included budget-aware planning and broader advisory features. Deliberately removed both to keep the AI safety layer manageable and the system auditable for rice and corn only.

Technical Verdict

Why the Hybrid Design Is the Right Engineering Choice

A pure LLM approach produces fluent text but cannot guarantee that fertilizer rates, planting windows, or pest thresholds are grounded in Philippine agricultural standards. A purely deterministic approach guarantees accuracy but produces plans too rigid and technical for a first-time farmer to use independently.

AniBot's hybrid architecture assigns each layer to its comparative strength: the knowledge database grounds the advice, the deterministic planner structures it, Gemma makes it intelligible, and the safety validator ensures nothing unsafe reaches a farmer's hands. Each layer is independently auditable — a requirement for any system deployed in a public agriculture office.

Engineering Rationale

Gemma 4's role in AniBot is deliberately bounded: it is a language improvement layer over a source-backed plan, not the source of the plan itself. This is not a limitation — it is the design. Precision in scope is what makes the system safe enough to put in front of a farmer who will act on what it says.

The result is an architecture that is offline-first, source-backed, locally runnable on a Raspberry Pi 5, kiosk-deployable in a municipal agriculture hub, and printable for farmers who need a physical copy to take into the field. The video demonstration shows the end-to-end user flow. This document explains the engineering decisions behind it.
